turns.content: I've read about this in the docs but still don't fully understand how fs::create_dir_all actually works compared to just calling create_dir repeatedly.

The key difference is that create_dir_all recursively creates all missing parent directories, whereas calling create_dir on each path individually will fail if a parent directory doesn't exist yet. For example, trying to call fs::create_dir("/a/b/c") when /b doesn't exist throws an error because the function expects only one level deep.
Ah okay so I need to manually check if parents exist? That sounds tedious for a script that builds nested configs.

Not really, fs::create_dir_all handles the recursion automatically. However, be aware it does not create files or symlinks, only actual directories. If you need to ensure specific permissions are set on all created dirs, you might want to combine this with std::os::unix::fs::DirBuilder for fine-grained control.
That makes sense, but what if I accidentally overwrite an existing directory instead of creating it? Is there a way to be more safe?

You can use the FromError trait on create_dir_all to catch errors gracefully. But remember that fs::remove_dir only removes a single directory, so for nested structures you must use remove_dir_all which walks down and deletes everything recursively.
Got it. Now I'm confused about read_dir - does it return files too or just directories?

fs::read_dir returns an iterator of DirEntry objects, and each entry contains both file_type flags and metadata like name and size. You can check the is_file flag on a DirEntry to distinguish between regular files and directories before deciding what to do.
So I need to loop through that iterator and call file_type.is_dir() for every entry? That seems like extra overhead compared to just listing paths.

Yes, you must inspect the DirEntry structure because read_dir doesn't guarantee sorted order or specific types. However, if performance is critical and you know all entries are directories, you could skip the check but be aware this risks silently ignoring errors.
Wait, I thought recursive walks were just calling read_dir on each directory? That sounds like it would explode in complexity if not careful.

Exactly, a simple loop over read_dir results will miss nested directories unless you explicitly recurse. The common pattern is to collect all DirEntry objects first, then filter by file_type.is_dir(), and only then call read_dir on those subdirectories.
Oh I see now! So the real issue was thinking that one read_dir call covers everything instead of realizing we need nested calls?

Precisely. Your understanding has shifted from flat iteration to recursive traversal. One final note: fs::create_dir_all is safe for most cases but remember it can create symlinks pointing to directories if you use that feature, which might be unexpected depending on your security requirements.
That's a good catch. So my final plan is: use create_dir_all for setup, read_dir with file_type checks for scanning, and remove_dir_all only when I'm sure of the content structure?
Yes, that is exactly right. Just ensure you never call remove on a path that still has active file descriptors open from other processes before deletion.
